name: Wheel of Fortune (X)
shortDescription: The Wheel of Fortune represents cycles, destiny, and turning points in life. It symbolizes the unpredictable nature of fate and the ever-changing flow of events.
description: The Wheel of Fortune, card number X in the Major Arcana of the Tarot, embodies the concept of cyclical change, destiny, and the unpredictable nature of life. It suggests that life is a series of ups and downs, fortunes and misfortunes, and that nothing stays the same forever. The wheel itself symbolizes the continuous turning of fate, with its revolutions bringing both good and bad luck. The card often depicts various figures clinging to the wheel, some rising and others falling, illustrating the shifting fortunes of individuals. The Wheel of Fortune is not simply about blind luck; it also acknowledges the influence of our actions and choices on our destiny. While we cannot control every event, we can influence the outcome through our decisions and how we respond to change. The card encourages us to embrace the inevitable changes in life, learn from our experiences, and adapt to new circumstances. When the Wheel of Fortune appears in a reading, it often signifies a significant turning point or a major shift in circumstances. It may indicate that a period of good luck or fortune is on the horizon, or it could warn of impending challenges. Regardless, the card reminds us to be prepared for change and to trust in the natural flow of life. The figures often depicted on the wheel, such as the Sphinx, Anubis, or Typhon, represent different aspects of destiny and the forces that shape our lives. The Wheel of Fortune is closely related to the concept of karma, suggesting that our past actions influence our present and future experiences. It emphasizes the importance of making wise choices and living in alignment with our values to create a positive destiny. It is also related to the World Card, as both signify the completion of a cycle, though the Wheel represents ongoing cycles while the World represents a final completion. It connects to the four fixed signs in astrology: Taurus (tarot_bull), Leo (tarot_lion), Scorpio/Eagle (tarot_eagle), and Aquarius/Man (tarot_man), representing the stability and grounding principles that can be found within the chaos of life's turning wheel.

sources.title: The Pictorial Key to the Tarot
sources.author: A.E. Waite

sources.title: Meditations on the Tarot: A Journey into Christian Hermeticism
sources.author: Anonymous

sources.title: Seventy-Eight Degrees of Wisdom: A Book of Tarot
sources.author: Rachel Pollack

worshipPractices.name: Chance Altars
worshipPractices.description: Followers create small altars dedicated to the Wheel, adorned with symbols of luck, chance, and personal turning points. Offerings might include dice, coins, or mementos representing significant life changes. Prayers focus on seeking guidance in navigating life's uncertainties and accepting both good and bad fortune.

worshipPractices.name: Ritualistic Games
worshipPractices.description: Devotees engage in games of chance as a form of worship, believing that the act of embracing uncertainty honors the Wheel's influence. These games are played with intention, focusing on the symbolism of risk and reward, and the acceptance of outcomes beyond one's control. Results are interpreted as messages or omens related to personal destiny.

festivals.name: The Turning Festival
festivals.timing: At the equinoxes (both spring and autumn)
festivals.description: A bi-annual celebration marking the changing seasons and the cyclical nature of life. Rituals involve spinning wheels, games of chance, and reflection on past events and future possibilities. Celebrants share stories of personal transformation and offer gratitude for both fortunate and challenging experiences.

festivals.name: The Day of Unpredictability
festivals.timing: An arbitrarily chosen day by an individual, believed to be a random turning point
festivals.description: On this day, believers intentionally step outside their comfort zones and embrace spontaneous decisions. They seek out new experiences, take calculated risks, and accept whatever outcomes may arise. The goal is to cultivate adaptability and a sense of trust in the unpredictable flow of life.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Wheel of Fortune's influence persists in contemporary art that explores themes of fate and chance. It also appears in literature and film as a symbol of unpredictable destiny, reminding us that life is a continuous cycle of change. The concept of 'karma' and cyclical returns in narratives are modern reflections of this influence.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: The Norns control fate and destiny, weaving the tapestry of life, dictating the cyclical nature of events and individual fortunes, much like the Wheel of Fortune.
Tyche is the Greek goddess of fortune, chance, and destiny, directly embodying the unpredictable changes and shifts in luck represented by the Wheel of Fortune.

The concept of Karma represents the cyclical nature of cause and effect, influencing one's destiny based on past actions, reflecting the Wheel of Fortune's theme of inevitable consequences and changing circumstances.

Fortuna, the Roman goddess of fortune, embodies the fickle nature of luck, often depicted with a wheel or cornucopia to symbolize the constant changes in prosperity and adversity represented by the Wheel of Fortune.

associations.name: Sphinx
associations.significance: The Sphinx atop the wheel represents knowledge and the riddles of fate, posing challenges to those who seek to understand their destiny.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Fire embodies the transformative energy that fuels the turning of the wheel, representing both creation and destruction as part of life's cycle.

associations.name: Ten
associations.significance: Ten signifies completion and new beginnings, as the end of a numerical cycle leads to the start of another, mirroring the Wheel's cyclical nature.

associations.name: Jupiter
associations.significance: Jupiter, often associated with luck and expansion, governs the Wheel, influencing the turning of fortune and the opportunities that arise.

associations.name: Karma
associations.significance: The Wheel embodies the principle of Karma, suggesting that actions have consequences and influence future circumstances, shaping one's destiny.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Gold symbolizes wealth and prosperity, reflecting the potential for good fortune and abundance that the Wheel can bring.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: The serpent descending the wheel can represent the inevitable downturns and challenges that accompany life's cyclical nature.

companions.name: Anubis
companions.description: In some interpretations, Anubis, the Egyptian god of the afterlife, is seen guiding souls through the cyclical journey of life and death represented by the Wheel. He oversees the transitions and changes inherent in the card's symbolism.

companions.name: Sphinx
companions.description: The Sphinx, often depicted atop the Wheel, symbolizes wisdom and riddles of fate. It represents the unchanging observer of the Wheel's turning, posing questions about destiny and free will.

companions.name: Typhon
companions.description: Often depicted descending on the Wheel, Typhon is a symbol of chaos and the destructive forces that can disrupt fate. Representing the unpredictable downsides of fortune, it is an opposing force to the Wheel's positive aspects.

companions.name: Hermes
companions.description: Hermes, as a messenger god, assists with adapting to the circumstances the Wheel of Fortune brings. He offers guidance and new opportunities.

extendedContent.title: Creation & Forging of the Wheel
extendedContent.content:
While the Tarot itself doesn't originate with a singular 'creation myth' for the Wheel of Fortune, we can extrapolate its origins from broader philosophical and religious concepts about fate and cyclical time. Ancient Greek mythology provides fertile ground; consider the Moirae, the three Fates who spun, measured, and cut the thread of life. Their influence over mortal existence mirrors the Wheel's control over fortune. The Mesopotamian concept of the 'Tablet of Destinies', held by gods like Anu and later Marduk, also reflects the idea of a preordained, yet potentially changeable, course of events. This tablet granted its holder dominion over cosmic order and the fate of all beings. 

Similarly, within Hindu cosmology, the concept of Samsara, the cycle of birth, death, and rebirth driven by karma, resonates strongly with the Wheel's cyclical nature. The turning of the Wheel can be seen as a symbolic representation of this unending cycle, where actions have consequences that dictate future experiences. The forging of the Wheel, therefore, isn't a literal crafting but rather a symbolic representation of the universe's inherent order and the forces that govern it. 

Ultimately, the 'creation' of the Wheel of Fortune lies in the human need to understand and grapple with the unpredictable nature of life. It's a tool for contemplating the interplay between free will and destiny, a visual representation of the forces that shape our experiences, whether they be divine, karmic, or simply the result of chance.

extendedContent.title: Powers & Properties of the Wheel
extendedContent.content:
The primary power attributed to the Wheel of Fortune is its ability to influence fate and destiny. It represents the turning of the tides, the unpredictable shifts in fortune that can elevate individuals to great heights or plunge them into despair. This power isn't necessarily malevolent; rather, it's impartial, distributing both blessings and challenges according to a cosmic order that is often beyond human comprehension. The Wheel's 'properties' extend to its representation of karmic cycles; actions taken in the past can have profound effects on future circumstances, echoing the principle of cause and effect. 

Furthermore, the Wheel symbolizes the ephemeral nature of all things. Nothing remains static; fortunes rise and fall, empires crumble, and even the most powerful individuals are subject to the vagaries of fate. This impermanence can be both terrifying and liberating. It reminds us to appreciate the present moment and to avoid becoming overly attached to material possessions or fleeting successes. The Wheel, therefore, possesses the power to instill both humility and resilience. 

Beyond direct influence over fate, the Wheel also grants insight. Contemplating the Wheel can help individuals understand the patterns and cycles in their lives, allowing them to anticipate potential challenges and capitalize on opportunities. It encourages a broader perspective, reminding us that even seemingly negative experiences can ultimately contribute to our growth and development.

extendedContent.title: Notable Wielders (Interpretations)
extendedContent.content:
The Wheel of Fortune, as a symbolic representation of fate, doesn't have literal 'wielders' in the traditional sense of a mythological artifact. However, various figures throughout mythology and history can be interpreted as embodying the principles represented by the Wheel. For example, the Greek goddess Tyche (Roman Fortuna) was the personification of luck and chance, constantly turning the wheel of fortune for individuals and cities. Her capricious nature and ability to bestow both blessings and curses perfectly align with the card's symbolism. 

Another interpretation of a 'wielder' could be Oedipus from Greek tragedy. His life was seemingly predetermined by prophecy, a force akin to the Wheel's influence. Despite his attempts to escape his fate, he ultimately fulfilled the oracle's predictions, demonstrating the power of destiny to shape human lives. However, Oedipus also demonstrates the complexities of free will versus fate, as his choices, though seemingly driven by the prophecy, contributed to his downfall. 

Even historical figures like Julius Caesar, whose rise to power and subsequent assassination highlight the fleeting nature of fortune, can be seen as being 'wielded' by the Wheel. His ambition and success ultimately led to his demise, illustrating the cyclical pattern of rise and fall. These figures, while not literally wielding the Wheel, serve as potent examples of the card's core themes in action.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism and Iconography
extendedContent.content:
The symbolism within the Wheel of Fortune card is rich and multifaceted. The wheel itself, the central image, represents the cyclical nature of life, with its constant turning signifying the ebb and flow of fortune. Often, figures are depicted clinging to the wheel, some rising and others falling, illustrating the unpredictable nature of fate. These figures can represent individuals, empires, or even abstract concepts like success and failure. The presence of animals, such as the sphinx, Anubis, and the serpent, further enriches the symbolism. 

The sphinx, often positioned at the top of the wheel, represents wisdom and knowledge, suggesting that understanding the cycles of life can lead to enlightenment. Anubis, the Egyptian god of embalming and the afterlife, is often depicted on the descending side of the wheel, symbolizing transition and the inevitable encounter with death. The serpent, winding its way around the wheel, represents change, transformation, and the hidden currents of fate. 

The letters inscribed on the wheel, often derived from Hebrew or other ancient alphabets, can be interpreted as representing the divine name or esoteric knowledge, hinting at a deeper understanding of the forces that govern the universe. The overall iconography of the Wheel of Fortune serves as a visual reminder of the impermanence of all things, the importance of adapting to change, and the need to embrace both the positive and negative aspects of life's cyclical journey.
